text.title: Real-World Match Examples and Antipatterns
text.summary: Product examples demonstrating correct language use, and antipatterns showing system-centric violations with rewrites.

example.note: Airbnb — domain language matching hotel mental models
example.code: "Check in" / "Check out" (not "Start date" / "End date") "Guests" (not "Users") "Listing" (not "Property record") "Your trips" (not "Booking history")

example.note: Gmail — physical mailbox metaphors users recognize
example.code: "Inbox" — physical mailbox concept "Archive" — storing for later, not deleting "Snooze" — sleep metaphor for timed reminders "Labels" — physical label stickers on folders

antipattern.description: Technical jargon as primary UI copy: "Configure SMTP parameters for outbound mail relay"
antipattern.reason: Requires technical knowledge the user does not have. Rewrite: "Set up email sending". The action is the same; the user does not need to know what SMTP is.
Confirms the system's action, not the user's goal. Rewrite: "Your booking is confirmed" — confirms what the user was trying to achieve.

antipattern.description: Inconsistent terminology across pages: "Sign in" on homepage, "Login" on mobile, "Log in" in email
antipattern.reason: Forces users to question whether these are the same action. Pick one term and apply it everywhere. Inconsistency is a heuristic #2 violation regardless of individual clarity.

antipattern.description: Locale-blind date format: displaying "03/04/2025" without locale detection
antipattern.reason: March 4 in US format is April 3 in European format. For financial, travel, and medical products, this is not just confusing — it can cause errors with real consequences. Use unambiguous formats or locale detection.

antipattern.description: Forced simplification of technical terms that users expect: changing "invoice" to "bill" in an accounting tool
antipattern.reason: Domain-expert users have precise vocabulary expectations. Over-simplifying removes signal. For expert-user products, "plain language" means the domain's language, not generic consumer language.

rule.statement: Extract actual user vocabulary from support tickets, user interview recordings, and search query logs before writing UI copy. Use the words users use, not words the team prefers.
rule.rationale: The most common source of heuristic #2 violations is team vocabulary leaking into UI copy. Users reveal their language in support requests and search queries — those are the ground truth.
